1.label: Question 1 (International/Global Coverage)
1.scenario: Your Company plans to come up with a Health Indemnity insurance product which aims to provide both domestic and international coverage.

i.question: Explain the factors that you need to consider while designing the international coverage.

Design Considerations: Target segment & selling process: USP vs travel insurance. [1]
Comparison with premium of existing travel products. [1]
Coverage scope: IPD/OPD, and traditionally travel-linked benefits (baggage/passport). [1]
PED coverage & Geographical scope (Global vs specific regions). [1]
Underwriting: Trigger points for medical tests and timing of optional cover purchase. [1]
Reinsurance and Capital requirements for high severity claims. [1]

ii.question: Explain the challenges associated with Global coverage proposition?

Key Challenges: Data scarcity for pricing without overseas experience. [1]
High potential claim liability in USD/Euro (severity risk). [1]
Synergy/Cannibalization with existing health or travel products. [1]
Claim administration: building a cashless network and language barriers. [1]
Currency Risk: Exchange rate volatility and asset-liability matching (ALM) restrictions. [1]
Affordability: High premiums could alienate certain market segments. [1]

iii.question: Discuss the ways to address some or all of the challenges?

Mitigation Strategies: Refer to external data: medical journals, government stats, and Reinsurer help. [1]
Differentiate USP: Offer as a renewal optional cover to avoid travel insurance overlap. [1]
Product restrictions: Co-pay/deductibles and limits on trip duration. [1]
Stricter underwriting for the global component. [1]
Tie-up with specialized International TPAs and re-pricing agencies. [1]
Monitor and re-file: Revision of rates if currency shifts long-term. [1]

iv.question: Explain the fundamental difference between global coverage and standard travel insurance (Features & Rating).

a) Product Features: PED: Travel products often have sub-limits for emergencies; Global products cover fully after waiting periods. [1]
Renewal: Travel is short-term/non-renewable; Global is lifelong renewable. [1]
Add-ons: Travel includes non-medical (baggage/passport); Global focus is medical. [1]
b) Rating Structure: Duration: Travel premiums depend on trip length; Global is a flat yearly renewable rate. [1]
Region: Travel is destination-specific; Global assumes a default Region Mix. [1]

v.question: Pricing: a) Domestic Data analysis, b) Maturity issues, c) Steps for Global risk premium. (4+4+6)

a) Domestic Data Analysis: Severity: Adjust for one-offs/IBNER/Inflation; remove design caps (room rent) to get true cost. [2]
Frequency: Adjust count for IBNR and analyze by rating factor cells (age/SUM). [2]
b) Maturity Considerations: Selection Effect: Initial durations show better experience due to recent underwriting. [1]
Waiting Periods: Claims vary by duration until temporary exclusions (cataract, etc.) fade. [1]
Selective Lapsing: Poor risks tend to stay while good risks migrate; duration must be modeled. [2]
c) Global Risk Premium Steps: Use cost multipliers for different regions (USA vs Asia) vs India. [2]
Adjust domestic claimed amounts by specific currency exchange rates. [1]
Segment into Travel-linked vs Planned Surgeries (Planned has higher loading). [2]
Factor in duration-outside-India to reduce the domestic component overlap. [1]

i.question: Pre-conditions for the success of a Universal Health Insurance Scheme.

Success Factors: Simplicity: Clear benefits and operations to build trust among the financially illiterate. [2]
Large Enrolment: Crucial for financial viability of low premiums without medical underwriting. [2]
Risk Equalisation: Pooling mechanism between insurers to ensure sustainability. [2]
Provider Contracting: Negotiated package rates (similar to RSBY) for cost control. [2]
Technology: Large-scale IT for fraud management and data sharing. [2]

ii.question: Inter-relation between Pricing, Underwriting, and PED coverage.

Trade-offs: Affordability vs Gatekeeping: Low underwriting requires waiting periods for PED. [2]
Premium Payer: If insured pays, higher awareness leads to higher utilization. [1]
Risk Pooling: Reduces the need for conservative initial premium margins. [1]
Consistency: Standardized features across insurers prevents selection against one player. [1]

iii.question: Five important actions the Government can take to ensure success.

Government Role: Awareness Campaigns: Government reach far exceeds individual insurers. [2]
Stakeholder Alignment: Bringing Banks, TPAs, and Providers into a unified system. [2]
Risk Pooling/Reinsurance: Gov as a backstop (Stop Loss) or capping excess profits. [2]
Subsidies: Partial/full premium funding for BPL and Senior Citizens. [2]
IT Infrastructure: Initial development/funding of the core technology architecture. [2]

i.question: Importance of branding and strategic product development.

Branding Strategy: Market Reach: Influence and educate customers to gain competitive edge. [1]
Expense reduction: Promote direct/online channels to save on commission. [1]
Differentiation: Unique market positioning clearly separated from competitors. [1]
Touch-points: Consistent brand guidelines across the entire product cycle. [1]

ii.question: Issues resulting from the recent brand campaign (XYZ Pharmacies).

Key Issues: Tier 1 concentration: Drug benefit unavailable in non-metro areas causing dissatisfaction. [1]
Regulatory Mis-selling: Promoting non-insurance benefits (drug discounts) improperly. [1]
Partner Dependency: Lapses in drugstore service damage the insurer's brand image. [1]
Marketing Over-run: High campaign costs exceeding budget without volume growth. [1]
Compliance: Penalties for not adhering to health insurance advertising guidelines. [1]

iii.question: Digital Marketing: Definition, Advantages, and Disadvantages.

Advantages: Personalization: Tailoring messages based on customer prefences/needs. [1]
Selection: Focus on good risks (e.g., active tech users). [1]
Efficiency: Wider reach at lower distribution expenses. [1]
Disadvantages: Complexity: Hard to explain nuanced benefits via pure digital self-service. [1]
Adverse Selection: Sophisticated users may better understand how to select against insurer. [1]
Cannibalization: May alienate existing traditional agents/brokers. [1]
Data Privacy: Increased risk of cyber-breaches and sensitive data leaks. [1]

iv.question: Immediate points for the Senior Management Committee regarding digital strategy.

Strategy Evaluation: Budget Allocation: Shift % from conventional to digital marketing. [1]
Technology Audit: Capability to adapt existing systems to web/apps. [1]
Channel Mix: Evaluation of social media vs paid search vs website blogs. [1]
Management: Need for a dedicated Digital Marketing Head. [1]
Product Choice: Deciding which specific products are suitable for digital-only launch. [1]
Risk Registry: Address regulatory and reputational risks early. [1]

v.question: Key risks of developing an 'Online-only' health insurance product.

Specific Risks: Demanding Segment: Digital youth expect instant/customized service; high grievance risk. [2]
Experience Uncertainty: These profiles differ from the historical 7-year book. [1]
Capital Strain: Huge initial IT investment for specialized portals. [1]
Pricing Comparison: Public prices allow competition to copy and undercut easily. [1]
Agent Friction: Existing offline channels may feel threatened and stop selling. [1]

vi.question: Financial Viability analysis for the digital channel.

DCF Approach: Gestation: 5-6 year projection timeframe (Discounted Cash Flow model). [2]
Revenue: Split between New Business and higher/lower persistency for digital. [2]
Expenses: Allocate set-up cost vs reducing incremental costs over volume. [2]
Claims: Factor in potentially different morbidity profiles for online buyers. [1]
Terminal Value: Estimate based on risk discount rate and growth assumptions. [2]
Capital: Include funding for losses and regulatory solvency (initial strain). [1]
